artifacts.content:
Hi Angie,

Finally sitting down for a minute. It’s been one of those winters where work and family are all tangled together, and that stupid 2007 plow (Unit 23) is at the center of too much of it.

You probably remember me venting last year about the trench collapse by South 6th & Maple on June 3, 2024 that sent Mark Jensen to the ER and turned into an OSHA citation and workers’ comp mess. I’m still dealing with follow‑up questions about that, and now Mark’s the same guy who calls me from Washington & 13th Street NW every time Unit 23 stalls out. On January 6, 2025 he blew up on the phone, said he was “sick of babysitting this piece of junk” and threatened to drag AFSCME Local 180 into it. I actually had to leave Grace’s counseling appointment at Northern Pines 20 minutes early to get him and the truck back to the Public Works garage, then spend the next day writing a memo for Emily in HR.

At the same time, Dad’s been in and out of Essentia–St. Joe’s with that mild stroke on April 3, 2025 and now rehab three mornings a week. I’m sneaking over to Baxter on long lunch breaks to help Mom with his exercises and meds, then coming back to City Hall to argue about why we need to replace an 18‑year‑old plow.

Sometimes it feels like every lane mile in Brainerd runs straight through our family. Anyway, how are things in St. Cloud? We still owe you a visit once hockey and snow season calm down.

Mike

Mom,

I wanted to write instead of just calling so I don’t forget anything. I know you’re still worried about Dad driving after the strokes. That January 9–12, 2025 snow emergency when he was in Essentia–St. Joseph’s for the second one really hammered that home. We were short‑staffed, I was juggling visits to his room, and we still racked up about $14,800 in unplanned overtime just trying to keep up with the storm.

That’s part of why I’m pushing so hard to get the new Freightliner 108SD ordered to replace Unit 23. We keep pouring money into that 2007 International, like the $7,800 frame and underbody corrosion work we did at Northland Truck Repair back in March 2024. Every time it dies, routes like College Drive and Maple Street fall behind. You remember April 2023, when Maple by Brainerd High wasn’t cleared before school and Karen gave me that written reprimand on May 3? I was already in a bad spot then, starting depression treatment at Lakewood Counseling Center, and I don’t want to repeat that cycle.

On top of it, Laura cutting back to part‑time at Essentia and the $9,300 we paid last October for Grace’s anxiety treatment at Lakes Area Behavioral Medicine means I can’t keep fronting little work expenses out of pocket like I did in 2022–23. The city has to fully fund this truck and its operating costs.

We’ll swing by Baxter Sunday after church to help with Dad’s meds and the walker. Tell him Evan’s getting more confident driving in the snow—carefully.

Love,
Mike

metadata.subject: Dad, the snowplows, and money stuff

artifacts.content:
Personal journal – late night at the dining room table

I keep replaying a string of storms in my head and wondering how much of this job is worth what it’s doing to everyone around me.

December 19, 2024: Unit 23 dies on College Drive right when Brainerd High is letting out. Evan texts that traffic is a mess and he and a couple of friends almost get rear‑ended on the slushy hill because cars are swerving around a disabled plow. I call Principal Karen Swanson from my truck and leave a voicemail apologizing, promising we’ll prioritize replacing that truck in the 2026 CER plan. Hard to sound calm when your own kid is in that line of cars.

February 12, 2025: hydraulic failure at South 7th & Maple, truck slides through the intersection and nearly hits a pedestrian pushing a stroller. I spend the next week writing a statement for Risk Manager Karen Olson and the League of Minnesota Cities adjuster, then start seeing Dr. Matthew Carlson at Essentia–Brainerd Clinic twice a month because I can’t stop seeing that stroller in my sleep.

Meanwhile Dad’s mild stroke on April 3, 2025 has me running from City Hall to Essentia–St. Joe’s, then to Baxter, then back to the Public Works garage to argue about an 18‑year‑old plow. It’s all the same story: we’re trying to do too much with equipment that should’ve been retired years ago, and the stress is spilling over into every corner of my life.

Note to self: bring this up with Dr. Carlson next visit.

metadata.title: Storm memories & why Unit 23 has to go

artifacts.content:
Event: Council prep block – snowplow replacement & winter incidents
Time: Monday, 2025-05-19, 1:30–3:00 p.m.
Location: Office – City Hall, Brainerd
Attendees: Michael Thompson

Notes:

Use this time to tighten up slides and talking points before the June council meeting. Need to weave together:

– Technical case for replacing Unit #23 (2007 International 7600, 178k miles, 10,400+ hours, corrosion, repeated hydraulic and electrical failures).

– Safety incidents that are still weighing on me: Dec 19, 2024 breakdown on College Drive by Brainerd High when Evan almost got rear‑ended; Feb 12, 2025 near‑miss at South 7th & Maple with the pedestrian and stroller; Jan 7–8, 2025 storm when the route by Brainerd High and Forestview lagged and Evan texted about nearly getting sideswiped leaving the student lot.

– Staff impacts: June 3, 2024 trench collapse at South 6th & Maple, OSHA citation, Mark Jensen’s workers’ comp claim; Jan 6, 2025 stall at Washington & 13th where Mark called from the cab furious and threatening an AFSCME Local 180 grievance while I was trying to sit through Grace’s counseling session at Northern Pines.

– Personal/family context (only if it comes up informally): Dad’s strokes (second one during the Jan 9–12, 2025 snow emergency at Essentia–St. Joe’s), current rehab schedule, me seeing Dr. Carlson at Essentia for anxiety.

Bring: incident timelines, maintenance logs, quote from Nuss for 2026 Freightliner 108SD, CER fund balance snapshot.

metadata.title: Council prep – Unit 23 replacement narrative
metadata.location: Brainerd City Hall – Public Works office

artifacts.content:
Laura,

Kids are finally in bed and I’m staring at the kitchen table covered in bills again, so I figured I’d write this out.

Ever since you cut back to part‑time at Essentia last fall and we got hit with that $9,300 bill for Grace’s anxiety treatment at Lakes Area Behavioral Medicine in October 2024, the margin has disappeared. I know we agreed I’d stop quietly covering little work costs—fuel top‑offs, a hose here and there—for the plow fleet like I did in 2022–23, but I still catch myself reaching for my own card.

It doesn’t help that Unit 23 keeps eating the city’s budget and our time. Remember the $7,800 corrosion repair at Northland Truck Repair in March 2024? Now we’re looking at ordering the 2026 Freightliner 108SD for $278,450, which at least is in the CER fund instead of our checkbook. On top of that, we had Dad’s second stroke during the January 9–12, 2025 storm and the $14,800 in overtime that went with being short‑staffed while I was running back and forth to Essentia–St. Joe’s.

I’m also still doing twice‑monthly appointments with Dr. Matthew Carlson at Essentia–Brainerd for anxiety since that Feb 12 near‑miss at South 7th & Maple. Between your shifts, my schedule, and Grace’s sessions at Northern Pines, it feels like half our life is spent in waiting rooms.

Let’s sit down Sunday afternoon—no hockey, no choir—and go through the budget together. I want to make sure we’re still on track for at least starting Grace’s braces at Lakes Area Dental next year.

Love you,
Mike

Running list – reasons I can’t keep patching Unit 23

1. Home driveway “repairs”: In January–March 2025 I actually had Evan and his buddy Tyler from Brainerd High’s auto tech program help me swap a leaking hydraulic line on Unit 23 in our driveway at 1842 Pineview. We did it on January 18, 2025 so I wouldn’t have to push another $1,200 emergency repair through the city right after we finished paying Laura’s $4,600 ER bill from Essentia in December 2024. Totally inappropriate to be wrenching on city equipment with teenagers at home, even if they loved it.

2. Family health: Dad’s mild stroke on April 3, 2025 and rehab at Essentia–St. Joe’s three mornings a week mean my “free” time is now shuttling between City Hall, Baxter, and the hospital. I can’t be the guy who covers for a failing truck by working 16–18 hour shifts and then showing up at my parents’ place on Birchwood Drive to help Mom manage his Parkinson’s symptoms.

3. Staff strain: February 10–11, 2025 storm on Oak Street—had to call Dan in at 10:30 p.m. from Good Samaritan Society–Bethany while he was sitting with his dad after a minor stroke, just to swap parts between Unit 23 and 19 so we could get Oak/Pineview cleared before school.

4. My own head: After the Feb 12 near‑miss at South 7th & Maple, I’m in Dr. Carlson’s office twice a month for anxiety. This isn’t just about metal and hydraulics anymore.

metadata.title: Why Unit 23 has to be replaced this year
metadata.location: Public Works office – Brainerd City Hall

artifacts.content:
Hey [Name],

Figured I’d answer your “how’s winter treating you up there?” with more than a text.

Short version: I’m tired.

Longer version: The old 2007 International (Unit 23) has turned into the villain of my life. December 21–23, 2024 it went down twice, and I ended up pulling my dad in for 12‑hour seasonal shifts even though he’s 74 and had that minor cardiac procedure at Essentia–Baxter not long before. Then on February 22–24, 2025, back‑to‑back storms plus repeated breakdowns meant 16–18 hour days for me. I missed two evenings I’d promised to spend at my parents’ place on Birchwood Drive helping Mom manage Dad’s early‑stage Parkinson’s and get him to PT at Essentia–Baxter Clinic. Angie texted that he fell in the kitchen and they couldn’t even get out because the side streets weren’t plowed yet.

At home, Evan’s driving now, which makes every storm personal. On December 19, 2024 Unit 23 died on College Drive right by Brainerd High as classes let out; he texted me about cars swerving around the disabled plow. Then in the January 7–8, 2025 storm, the route by the high school and Forestview lagged again, and he nearly got sideswiped leaving the student lot.

All of that, plus the February 12 near‑miss at South 7th & Maple with the stroller, finally pushed me into seeing a cardiologist last April for a stress test and hypertension check, and now a therapist, Dr. Carlson at Essentia–Brainerd, for anxiety.

On the plus side, ice fishing on North Long Lake is still good therapy when I can sneak out. You should come up sometime and see what real winter looks like.

Mike
